coredata.dc:title: Polymer nanoparticle-based ratiometric fluorescent probe for imaging Hg2+ ions in living cells
coredata.dc:creator: Wang, Hong
Zhang, Peisheng
Chen, Jian
Li, Ya
Yu, Maolin
Long, Yunfei
Yi, Pinggui
coredata.dc:description: Mercury pollutant brings huge threat to human health and the environment because of its bioaccumulation and permanent damage. Thus, highly selective and ratiometric detection and imaging of Hg2+ in organisms is quite important. Herein, we report on a polymer nanoparticle-based ratiometric fluorescent probe (NP3) for highly selective recognition of Hg2+ in totally aqueous solution and imaging in living cells. This probe comprises the reference fluorescent dye (4-ethoxy-9-allyl-1,8-naphthalimide: EANI), which resides in the core of nanoparticle, and the Hg2+-recognition group (fluorescein derivative: AEMH-FITC) on the surface of nanoparticle. It displays distinct dual emissions at 432nm and 528nm under a single-wavelength excitation. The fluorescence of the FITC moieties can be effectively quenched by Hg2+ due to photoinduced electron transfer (PET) mechanism, while that of the EANI remains constant, resulting in a ratiometric fluorescent detection of Hg2+. The as-prepared NP3 not only shows favorable water dispersibility, excellent long-term photostability, but also exhibits highly selective ratiometric detection of Hg2+ over other interfering ions (such as Cu2+, Na+, Mg2+, Zn2+, Co2+, Mn2+, K+, Ca2+, Fe3+, Cd2+, Ag+, Fe2+), and quite low detection limit (∼75nM). Furthermore, intracellular fluorescence imaging experiments demonstrate that NP3 has good permeability of cell-membrane, and is able to facilitate visualization of Hg2+ changes in living cells.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Polymer nanoparticle
Ratiometric fluorescent probe
Photoinduced electron transfer (PET)
Hg2+
Living cell imaging

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Mechanism of NP3 responding to Hg2+
Ratiometric fluorescent sensing of Hg2+ in water

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency-matched-string: Scientific Research Foundation for the Returned Overseas Chinese Scholars

National Natural Science Foundation of China

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: We gratefully acknowledge the financial support by NSFC (Project no. 51373002, 51603067), Scientific Research Foundation for the Returned Overseas Chinese Scholars, Project funded by China Postdoctoral Science Foundation (Project no. 2014M550418, 2015T80867), and Open Project Program of State Key Laboratory of Chemo/Biosensing and Chemometrics (Project no. 2013008).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic illustration for the acceptor’s structure and proposed detection mechanism toward Hg2+.

(A) Fluorescence titration spectra of NP3 (0.50mg/mL) in buffer solution (pH 7.0) upon gradual addition of Hg2+ (0∼50μM); (B) Fluorescence intensity ratio (I528/I432) of NP3, (0.50mg/mL) as a function of Hg2+ concentration in buffer solution (pH 7.0). (λex =385nm).

(A) Fluorescence intensity ratio (I528/I432) for NP3 (0.50mg/mL) in buffer solution (pH 7.0) in the presence of 10μM Hg2+ or 20μM of various metal ions. Blank is 0μM Hg2+; (B) Fluorescence intensity ratio (I528/I432) for NP3 (0.78mg/mL) in buffer solution (pH 7.0) in the presence of 10μM Hg2+ together with 20μM various coexisting metal cations. Blank is 0μM Hg2+.

(I) Confocal fluorescence microscopy images of NP3 (0.05mg/mL) in HeLa cells before and after treatment with 10μM Hg2+ for 30min. The images of the first column (A, F) exhibit the corresponding bright field images. The second (B, G) and third (C, H) column were collected in blue channel (420–490nm) and green channel (500–560nm) with the excitation wavelength 405nm, respectively. The forth column (D, I) exhibits the overlay of green channel and blue channel. The images of the five columns (the ratio channel) were acquired by Image pro software. The color strip on the right shows the pseudo color change with Hg2+; (II): Quantification of fluorescence intensities for group (E) and group (J), respectively. (For interpretation of the references to colour in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)
Schematic illustration of preparation of NP3 and their application as ratiometric fluorescent probe for Hg2+ detection.
Determination of mercury ion ion in drinking water and tap water.

head.ce:title: Polymer nanoparticle-based ratiometric fluorescent probe for imaging Hg2+ ions in living cells

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Key Laboratory of Theoretical Organic Chemistry and Functional Molecule of Ministry of Education, Hunan Provincial Key Laboratory of Controllable Preparation and Functional Application of Fine Polymers, Hunan Province College Key Laboratory of QSAR/QSPR, School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Hunan University of Science and Technology, Xiangtan, Hunan, 411201, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Key Laboratory of Theoretical Organic Chemistry and Functional Molecule of Ministry of Education
Hunan Provincial Key Laboratory of Controllable Preparation and Functional Application of Fine Polymers
Hunan Province College Key Laboratory of QSAR/QSPR
School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering
Hunan University of Science and Technology

ce:footnote.ce:note-para: These authors contributed equally to this work.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Mercury pollutant brings huge threat to human health and the environment because of its bioaccumulation and permanent damage. Thus, highly selective and ratiometric detection and imaging of Hg2+ in organisms is quite important. Herein, we report on a polymer nanoparticle-based ratiometric fluorescent probe (NP3) for highly selective recognition of Hg2+ in totally aqueous solution and imaging in living cells. This probe comprises the reference fluorescent dye (4-ethoxy-9-allyl-1,8-naphthalimide: EANI), which resides in the core of nanoparticle, and the Hg2+-recognition group (fluorescein derivative: AEMH-FITC) on the surface of nanoparticle. It displays distinct dual emissions at 432nm and 528nm under a single-wavelength excitation. The fluorescence of the FITC moieties can be effectively quenched by Hg2+ due to photoinduced electron transfer (PET) mechanism, while that of the EANI remains constant, resulting in a ratiometric fluorescent detection of Hg2+. The as-prepared NP3 not only shows favorable water dispersibility, excellent long-term photostability, but also exhibits highly selective ratiometric detection of Hg2+ over other interfering ions (such as Cu2+, Na+, Mg2+, Zn2+, Co2+, Mn2+, K+, Ca2+, Fe3+, Cd2+, Ag+, Fe2+), and quite low detection limit (∼75nM). Furthermore, intracellular fluorescence imaging experiments demonstrate that NP3 has good permeability of cell-membrane, and is able to facilitate visualization of Hg2+ changes in living cells.

ce:section.ce:para: Mercury (Hg) pollutant brings a great threat to human health and the environment due to its bioaccumulation and permanent damage in central nervous and endocrine systems [1,2]. As its general ion form, Hg2+ can easily pass through biological membranes and lead to prenatal brain damage, serious cognitive and motion disorders, and Minamata disease [3,4]. In view of many common diseases related to Hg2+, it is of significant interest to develop a simple and rapid method for Hg2+ detection in environment and biological samples [5].

So far, several traditional analytical techniques including atomic absorption spectroscopy (AAS) [6], inductively coupled plasma mass spectrometry (ICPMS) [7], X-ray absorption spectroscopy [8], surface enhanced Raman scattering (SERS) [9], electrochemical [10] and colorimetric [11] have been established to determine Hg2+ concentration, Despite of these methods (AAS, ICPMS, X-ray absorption spectroscopy, SERS and electrochemical) provide sensitive and selective analysis, they require sophisticated instrumentation and complicated sample preparation. The colorimetric method is free of the above problems and it requires minimal instrumentations, making on-site real-time sensing even easier. However, it is still not suitable for in situ detection for living biosystems (live cells and tissues). Compared to traditional analytical techniques, fluorescence spectroscopy has become a powerful tool for sensing metal ions because of its non-ionizing radiation damage, simple operation and high sensitivity and selectivity [12–58]. To date, a variety of fluorescent sensors based on a single emission signal (“turn-on” or “turn-off”) for sensing Hg2+ have been reported [24–35]. However, one fluorescence emission signal-based measurements are readily perturbed by some limitations, such as microenvironment, sensor concentration, photobleaching and excitation intensity, etc [36–38]. By contrast, the ratiometric fluorescent sensors based on two emission signals at two distinct wavelengths can greatly alleviate the above limitations of one emission signal-based measurements [36–38].

In recent years, various ratiometric fluorescent sensors including small organic molecules [39–46], quantum dots (QDs) [47–49] and polymer particles [50–57] have been developed and attracted much attention in sensitive detection of Hg2+. The small organic molecules-based ratiometric fluorescent sensors, which suffer from less satisfactory water solubility, can barely work in the water-organic mixed solvents and easily tend to aggregate because of the π-π interactions between the chromophores [5]. Application of QDs-based ratiometric fluorescent sensors in living cell imaging is hampered by their “blink”, cytotoxicity and incompatibility within aqueous media [58]. To address these problems, polymer particles-based ratiometric fluorescent sensors have been received growing interest due to some outstanding properties, such as good water solubility/dispersibility, improved biocompatibility, tunable structure and function, excellent photostability [59,60]. Undoubtedly, these properties are also suitable for cellular Hg2+ imaging and tracking. Nevertheless, polymer particles-based ratiometric fluorescent sensors for imaging Hg2+ in living cells are still rare. Therefore, the development of polymer particles-based fluorescence sensor, towards ratiometric detection of intracellular Hg2+ with high accuracy and selectivity, remains challenging and is of crucial importance.

In this study, we developed a polymer nanoparticles-based ratiometric fluorescent probe (NP3) for highly selective and sensitive detection of Hg2+ in totally aqueous media and imaging in living cells. The probe was consisted of a hydrophobic dye 4-ethoxy-9-allyl-1,8-naphthalimide (EANI), which covalently incorporated into the core of nanoparticle, as the reference fluorescent dye, and a hydrophilic dye fluorescein derivative (AEMH-FITC), which covalently linked to the particle surface, as the Hg2+-recognition group. The as-prepared NP3 possessed distinct dual emission peaks at 432nm and 528nm under a single excitation wavelength. For this NP3 system, the presence of Hg2+ can effectively quench of the fluorescence of the FITC due to the PET effect, while the blue fluorescence of the EANI keeps constant, resulting in a ratiometric fluorescent sensing of Hg2+ in totally aqueous media and in living cells, as illustrated in Scheme 1 .

The surfactant cetyltrimethyl ammonium chloride (CTAC, 99.0%), n-hexadecane (HD, 99%), 2-aminoethyl methacrylate hydrochloride (AEMH 90%), fluorescein isothiocyanate (FITC 96%) were used as received from Sigma-Aldrich. The phenolic inhibitor in methyl methacrylate (MMA, Aldrich) was removed by washing three times with 10% sodium hydroxide solution and deionized water, and then purified by vacuum distillation. Azobisisobutyronitrile (AIBN, 99.99%, Aldrich) was recrystallized from ethanol and dried under vacuum. The double-distilled water used in this work was further purified with a Milli-Q system. Other reagents were analytical reagents and used without further purification. Nitrate salts of metal ions (Cu2+, Na+, Mg2+, Zn2+, Co2+, Mn2+, K+, Ca2+, Fe3+, Ni+, Cd2+, Ag+, Fe2+, Hg2+) were obtained from Aldrich. 4-Ethoxy-9-allyl-1,8-naphthalimide (EANI) were prepared according to the previously published procedures [58].

A mixture containing the monomer (MMA, 0.5g), hydrophobic dyes (EANI see Table S1), polymerization initiator (AIBN, 0.025g), and hydrophobe (HD, 0.075g) was added to 10mL water solution with emulsifier (CTAC, 0.1g) and AEMH (0.08g) and stirred (1000r/min) for 10min, then, the mixture was ultrasonicated for 15min (JY92-IIN) to obtain a stable miniemulsion. The mixture was cooled in an ice-bath during ultrasonication to avoid overheated. The resulting miniemulsion was put into a 50mL flask equipped with a condenser, which was immersed in an oil bath with a thermostat. The miniemulsion was polymerized at 75°C for 3h.

Subsequently, the as-prepared nanoparticles dispersion was transferred into a 25mL round bottom flask. After the pH value of the dispersion was adjusted to 7.5. Subsequently, ca. 5mg of FITC was added to the dispersion. The mixture was stirred at room temperature for 2days. The excess of FITC was removed by dialysis through a porous cellulose membrane (MWCO 3500) toward an aqueous solution of CTAC (10wt%). A stable NP3 dispersion was obtained (Scheme 1). The other samples were prepared by varying the contents of EANI and FITC (Table S1).

The effect of NP3 on cell proliferation in HeLa cells was performed via the CellTiter 96 AQueous One Solution Cell Proliferation Assay. First, cells were seeded at a density of 5.0×103 cells per well in 96-well plates. Then, cells were dealt with various diluted samples of NP3 in the presence of 10% serum. After 24h of incubation, 96-well plates were washed with PBS buffer for three times, and incubated for another 1h at 37°C with fresh medium containing CellTiter 96 AQueous One Solution. Thereafter, the absorbance of each plate was measured via a Thermo Scientific Multiskan MK3Microplate Reader (Thermo Fisher, USA) at 492nm.

The cells were stained firstly with the fresh cell growth medium supplemented with NP3 (0.05mg/mL) for 3h, then the cells were washed with PBS for three times and incubated for another 30min with 10μM Hg2+. Then, HeLa cells were incubated with 1mL of fresh medium for fluorescence imaging. All fluorescence images were acquired using an oil immersion objective (100×, NA 1.3) on an Olympus FV1000 confocal laser scanning fluorescence microscope containing an Olympus IX81 inverted microscope. Excitation: 405nm, blue emission collected: 420–490nm, green emission collected: 500–560nm.

1H NMR spectra was recorded on a Bruker AV-II 500MHz NMR spectrometer. MS was conducted with a Finnigan LCQ Advantage MAX mass spectrometer. The nanoparticle diameters were determined by a Malvern Nano-ZS90 instrument and their morphology was observed with an atomic force microscope (AFM, Bruker Dimension Icon) in the tapping mode. UV–vis spectra were recorded on a Shimadzu UV-2501PC spectrophotometer at room temperature (298K). Fluorescence spectra were recorded on a Shimadzu RF-5301PC fluorescence spectrophotometer at room temperature.

In this study, NP3 was prepared as follows: firstly, the reference fluorescent dye (EANI) contained nanoparticle dispersion was prepared via a facile one-pot miniemulsion polymerization; Subsequently, with abundant amino groups on their surfaces, the FITC can be covalently linked onto the particle surface to form the Hg2+-recognition group (AEMH-FITC) under mild condition, as depicted in Scheme 1. The amount of the EANI and FITC in the nanoparticle dispersion can be calculated according to our previous paper [58]. It is obvious that covalent linkage supported by 1H NMR can effectively avoid the dye leakage (Fig. S1), and the average diameter of nanoparticles has no obvious changes before and after grafting of FITC (Table S1). A represent atomic force microscopy (AFM) image for sample NP3 after grafting was shown in Fig. S2. It can be seen that most of the nanoparticles were discrete, and spherical shape with an average diameter of 61nm, which was consistent with that determined by dynamic light scattering (DLS) (Table S1).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Mechanism of NP3 responding to Hg2+
ce:section.ce:para: The fluorescence quenching mechanism of fluorescein toward Hg2+ is presumably due to photoinduced electron transfer (PET) from Hg2+-ligand coordination to fluorescein fluorophore (Fig. 1 ). To further confirm the mechanism of NP3 responding to Hg2+, the analogous compound, 2-(6-hydroxy-3-oxo-3H- xanthen-9-yl)-5-(3-(2-hydroxyethyl)-thioureido)benzoic acid (HEFITC, Fig. 1) was synthesized, and the spectral properties of HEFITC were systematically studied (Fig. S3). As seen in Fig. S3, HEFITC showed a prominent absorption peak at 490nm and a strong emission band at 518nm. However, with addition of Hg2+, the absorption at 490nm slightly decreased, while a largely decreased emission band at 518nm can be observed. In addition, the sensing mechanism can also be supported by a mass spectrometry (MS) (Fig. S4). Specifically, MS analysis of a mixture of HEFITC and 50 equivalents of Hg2+ in buffer solution (pH 7.0) after stirring for 30min contained a peak at m/z 449.1, which corresponding to the [HEFITC-Hg2+-H]+. This demonstrated the interaction of Hg2+-sensor, and confirmed the Hg2+-induced fluorescence change of the PET process.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Ratiometric fluorescent sensing of Hg2+ in water
ce:section.ce:para: The interaction between NP3 and Hg2+ in buffer solution (pH 7.0) was studied by fluorescence spectroscopy, as revealed in Fig. 2 A. In the absence of Hg2+, NP3 showed two distinct emission peaks at 432nm and 528nm, which were ascribed to the fluorescence emission of EANI and FITC, respectively. Upon the addition of Hg2+, the intensity of green emission from the fluorescein continuously and dramatically decreased, while the fluorescence intensity of EANI exhibited no obvious change.

Based on above results, it is obvious that the NP3 can rationally detect Hg2+ through a ratiometric fluorescence approach. In addition, we also established a working curve by plotting the fluorescence intensity ratio (I528/I432) versus Hg2+ concentration. As illustrated in Fig. S5, the fluorescence intensity ratio (I528/I432) gradually reduced with the increase of the concentration of Hg2+. By plotting the logarithmic of fluorescence intensity ratio (I528/I432) versus Hg2+ concentration, a good linear relationship was obtained between the logarithmic of fluorescence intensity ratio (I528/I432) and the Hg2+ concentration in the range 0–10μM (Pearson correlation coefficient (r)=−0.9957) (Fig. 2B). Meanwhile, the detection limit has been determined as 75nM (15ppb) based a signal-to-noise ratio (S/N=3), which is sensitive to detect total Hg2+ levels in human urine (0.4–2.0mg/L), blood (1.6–44μg/L) and some fishes suspected Hg2+ contamination [61]. In addition, the relative standard deviation (RSD) of the detection limit was calculated as 1.45%, indicating that the probe (NP3) has an excellent reproducibility.

Selectivity is one important parameter for evaluating the performance of a sensing system. The selectivity assay for NP3 towards Hg2+ was evaluated by employing a number of putative metal cation interferants, such as Cu2+, Na+, Mg2+, Zn2+, Co2+, Mn2+, K+, Ca2+, Fe3+, Ni2+, Cd2+, Ag+, Fe2+ and the results are shown in Fig. 3 A.

It is clear that the fluorescence intensity ratio (I528/I432) variation for NP3 is significantly reduced upon the addition of Hg2+. In comparison, no obvious change in I528/I432 can be observed in the presence of other analytes. Furthermore, competition experiments with the interferents were also performed (Fig. 3B). It is obvious that the co-existence of these metal cations has negligible interfering effect on Hg2+ sensing. Together with the data presented in Fig. 3, these results indicated that the nanosensor system could potentially be used to detect Hg2+ in a complex sample matrix containing interfering metal ions.

Moreover, the NP3 at pH 7.0 was continuously irradiated under a 15W 365nm UV lamp and I528/I432 was also recorded, as given in Fig. S6. The result demonstrated that less than 8% of the initial fluorescence intensity ratios (I528/I432) were lost after 90min of irradiation, indicating that the NP3 has sufficient photostability for potential biological imaging applications. As shown in Fig. S7, the fluorescence intensity ratio (I528/I432) of NP3 (0.78mg/mL) altered less than 6% even after storing for 49days, indicating that excellent long-term fluorescence stability as well as the negligible dye leakage.

It has been well documented that iodide ion (I−) was usually recognized toward Hg2+ to form HgI2 due to the stronger binding of Hg2+ and I− [41,62]. The fluorescence intensity ratio (I528/I432) for the NP3 pretreated with Hg2+ was measured in the presence of various amounts of I−, as shown in Fig. S8. It was evident from Fig. S8 that the I528/I432 variation of NP3 treated with Hg2+ (10μM) was significantly reduced compared to that of NP3 itself. Nevertheless, the I528/I432 for the NP3 pretreated with Hg2+ (10μM) has no obvious change in the different concentration of I− (10μM, 20μM, 30μM, 40μM). The results indicated that I− cannot be used to reversibly recognized towards Hg2+ in this nanosensor system.

For practical applicability, the suitable pH range for the NP3 system was also investigated. As depicted in Fig. S9, in the absence of Hg2+, the I528/I432 of NP3 dispersion was firstly increased and then remained fairly stable as the increasing pH. In the presence of Hg2+, a remarkable reduce in I528/I432 was observed. It also can be found that the NP3 dispersion exhibited relative low ratios which remained fairly stable from pH 7 to 9. These data established that the NP3 could serve as a ratiometric fluorescent sensor for Hg2+ under physiological pH conditions.

In addition, the temperature dependence of the I528/I432 of the NP3 dispersion in the presence or absence of Hg2+ was also recorded. Fig. S10 illustrated that the NP3 can work well under the various temperature. The above results indicated that temperature has negligible effect on the recongnition ability of NP3 to Hg2+.

The prominent spectral features of NP3 prompted us to test its potential applications for Hg2+ ratiometric fluorescence imaging in living cells. Before we applied the NP3 in cellular imaging, MTT assays were carried out to evaluate the cytotoxicity of PN to HeLa cells. As displayed in Fig. S11, with 24h incubation, no obvious reduction in cell viability can be observed for cells treated with the NP3 even at high concentrations (up to 0.15mg/mL), confirming it can be used for cell imaging.

To perform the feasibility of cellular Hg2+ sensing of NP3, the whole cells incubation conditions were controlled to maintain a fixed intracellular pH condition so as to make sure that the fluorescence changes are specific responsive to Hg2+. Subsequently, the cells prestained with NP3 were further incubated with Hg2+ (10μM) for 30min and washed, and the ratiometric confocal fluorescence cellular imaging experiment for Hg2+ was then carried out. As shown in Fig. 4 , the fluorescence from the green channel in cells decreases with increasing Hg2+ concentration, whereas that from the blue channel hardly alters. The ratio channel, obtained based on the above two channels, displays a characteristic Hg2+-dependent signal (Fig. 4II). The preliminary experimental results demonstrated that NP3 could be used for ratiometric imaging of Hg2+ in the living cells. In the future, our main goal is to use the ratiometric chemosensors for real-time monitoring of inorganic Hg2+ uptake by cells and its distribution concentration within cells.

To further investigate the practicability of the probe in usual water sample, NP3 was successfully applied to detecting of Hg2+ in several tap water and drinking water. As illustrated in Table 1 , the RSD for all assays are less than 0.34%, and recoveries are in the range of 87% to 100%. These results indicated this sense system (NP3) has a good precision accuracy for the selective recognition of Hg2+ in real aqueous samples.

In addition, the comparison of probe in this work and some previously reported Hg2+-based fluorescent probes are listed, as shown in Table S2. It is noteworthy to point out that the probe herein reveals excellent water-dispersibility, high selectivity, low detection limit, ratiometric detection. Moreover, it can be used to ratiometric image Hg2+ inside live cells.

In summary, we have successfully developed a polymer nanoparticle-based fluorescent ratiometric probe (NP3) for selectively detection of Hg2+ in totally aqueous media and imaging in living cells, via covalently incorporating the reference fluorescent dye (EANI) into the particle core, and covalently linking the Hg2+-recognition group (AEMH-FITC) onto the surface. Upon addition of Hg2+, the fluorescence of the FITC was effectively quenched due to the PET mechanism, while the fluorescence of the EANI kept constant, resulting in a ratiometric fluorescent detection of Hg2+. The as-prepared NP3 showed good water dispersibility, excellent long-term photostability, high selectivity and low detection limit (∼75nM). Moreover, intracellular fluorescence imaging experiments indicated that the probe held good permeability of cell-membrane; and enabled visualization of Hg2+ in living cells.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: We gratefully acknowledge the financial support by NSFC (Project no. 51373002, 51603067), Scientific Research Foundation for the Returned Overseas Chinese Scholars, Project funded by China Postdoctoral Science Foundation (Project no. 2014M550418, 2015T80867), and Open Project Program of State Key Laboratory of Chemo/Biosensing and Chemometrics (Project no. 2013008).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found, in the online version, at http://dx.doi.org/10.1016/j.snb.2016.09.177.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: The following is Supplementary data to this article:

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Sources and remediation for mercury contamination in aquatic systems-a literature review
The toxicology of mercury-current exposures and clinical manifestations
Visible study of mercuric ion and its conjugate in living cells of mammals and plants
Tools and tactics for the optical detection of mercuric ion
Determination of total mercury in human hair and animal fur by combustion atomic absorption spectrometry

Measurement of mercury species in whole blood using speciated isotope dilution methodology integrated with microwave-enhanced solubilization and spike equilibration headspace-solid-phase microextraction, and GC-ICP-MS analysis
Microprobe techniques for speciation analysis and geochemical characterization of mine environments: the mercury district of almadén in Spain
Determination of mercury(II) by surface-enhanced raman scattering spectroscopy based on thiolfunctionalized silver nanoparticles
Highly selective chromogenic and redox or fluorescent sensors of Hg2+ in aqueous environment based on 1,4-disubstituted azines
Colorimetric detection of mercuric ion (Hg2+) in aqueous media using DNA-functionalized gold nanoparticles
A logic gate-based fluorescent sensor for detecting H2S and NO in aqueous media and inside live cells
An ICT-based ratiometric fluorescent probe for hydrazine detection and its application in living cells and in vivo
Far-red to near infrared analyte-responsive fluorescent probes based on organic fluorophore platforms for fluorescence imaging
Design strategies for water-soluble small molecular chromogenic and fluorogenic probes
Recent progress in the development of fluorescent: luminescent and colorimetric probes for detection of reactive oxygen and nitrogen species
The development of fluorescent probes for visualizing intracellular hydrogen polysulfides
A ratiometric fluorescent formaldehyde probe for bioimaging applications
Far-red and near-infrared AIE-active fluorescent organic nanoprobes with enhanced tumor-targeting efficacy: shape-specific effects
One-pot production of fluorescent surface-labeled polymeric nanoparticles via miniemulsion polymerization with bodipy surfmers
FRET-mediated pH-responsive dual fluorescent nanoparticles prepared via click chemistry
Efficient rhodamine-thiosemicarbazide- based colorimetric/fluorescent ‘turn-on’ chemodosimeters for the detection of Hg2+ in aqueous samples
Water-soluble gold nanoclusters-based fluorescence probe for highly selective and sensitive detection of Hg2+
Carbon dots-based fluorescent probe for trace Hg2+ detection in water sample
A novel rhodamine-based colorimetric and fluorescent sensor for Hg2+ in water matrix and living cell
Spinach-extracted chlorophyll-a modified peanut shell as fluorescence sensors for selective detection of Hg2+ in water
An AIE active probe for specific sensing of Hg2+ based on linear conjugated bis-Schiff base
A fluorescence turn-on probe based on rhodamine derivative and its functionalized silica material for Hg2+-selective detection
Thymine-covalently decorated, AIEE-type conjugated polymer as fluorescence turn-on probe for aqueous Hg2+
A highly selective colorimetric and long-wavelength fluorescent probe for Hg2+
Dual signaling of Hg2+ ions by selective cleavage of thiophosphinated rhodol
Highly photoluminescent carbon dots-based fluorescent chemosensors for sensitive and selective detection of mercury ions and application of imaging in living cells
Nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dots: facile synthesis and application as a turn-off fluorescent probe for detection of Hg2+ ions
Energy transfer cassettes based on organic fluorophores: construction and applications in ratiometric sensing
Small molecule-based ratiometric fluorescence probes for cations anions, and biomolecules
FRET-based small-molecule fluorescent probes: rational design and bioimaging applications
A ratiometric fluorescent probe based on FRET for imaging Hg2+ ions in living cells
A colorimetric and ratiometric fluorescent probe for mercury (II) in lysosome
A ratiometric fluorescent chemosensor for Hg2+ based on FRET and its application in living cells
A dansyl-rhodamine ratiometric fluorescent probe for Hg2+ based on FRET mechanism
A phenothiazine-rhodamine ratiometric fluorescent probe for Hg2+ based on FRET and ICT
A new ratiometric fluorescent chemodosimeter based on an ICT modulation for the detection of Hg2+
Detection of Hg2+ by a FRET ratiometric fluorescent probe based on a novel BODIPY-RhB system
A new coumarin–rhodamine FRET system as an efficient ratiometric fluorescent probe for Hg2+ in aqueous solution and in living cells
Ratiometric fluorescence detection of mercuric ion based on the nanohybrid of fluorescence carbon dots and quantum dots
A ratiometric fluorescence sensor for highly selective and sensitive detection of mercuric ion
Determination of trace Hg2+ ions based on the fluorescence resonance energy transfer between fluorescent brightener and CdTe quantum dots
A FRET-based ratiometric sensor for mercury ions in water with multi-layered silica nanoparticles as the scaffold
FRET-Based ratiometric detection system for mercury ions in water with polymeric particles as scaffolds
A nanoparticle-supported fluorescence resonance energy transfer system formed via layer-by-layer approach as a ratiometric sensor for mercury ions in water
Highly selective fluorescence sensing of mercury ions over a broad concentration range based on mixed polymeric micelles
Thermoresponsive core cross-Linked micelles for selective ratiometric fluorescent detection of Hg2+Ions
Ratiometric fluorescence nanoparticles polymer detection of mercury ions in water by conjugated
Analyte-reactive amphiphilic thermoresponsive diblock copolymer micelles-based multifunctional ratiometric fluorescent chemosensors
Fluorescent mesoporous silica nanoparticles functionalized graphene oxide: a facile FRET-based ratiometric probe for Hg2+
Design and synthesis of FRET-mediated multicolor and photoswitchable fluorescent polymer nanoparticles with tunable emission properties
Engineering responsive polymer building blocks with host-guest molecular recognition for functional applications
Stimuli-Responsive tertiary amine methacrylate-based block copolymers: synthesis: supramolecular self-assembly and functional applications
Guidance for Identifying Populations at Risk from Mercury Exposure
Phenothiazine-aminothiourea-Hg(II) ensemble-based fluorescence turn-on toward iodide in aqueous media and imaging application in live cells

ce:biography.ce:simple-para: Hong Wang (male) is currently a junior in the School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Hunan University of Science and Technology, researching chemosensors.

Peisheng Zhang (male) is currently a lecturer in the School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Hunan University of Science and Technology, Xiangtan, China. His primary research interests include biosensing and chemometrics. He has published more than 10 papers in Biomaterials, Chemical Communications, Polymer Chemistry, and so on.

Jian Chen (male) is currently an associate professor in the School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Hunan University of Science and Technology, Xiangtan, China. His primary research interests include fluorescent nanomaterials, energy transfer in colloides, chemosensors and biosensors. he has published more than 20 papers in Small, Chemical Communications, Macromolecules, Polymer Chemistry, Nanotechnology, Journal of Physical Chemistry B, and so on.

Ya Li (female) received her bachelor’s degree in 2013 and is currently a postgraduate in the School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Hunan University of Science and Technology, researching chemosensors and biosensors. She has publised two paper in Sensors and Actuators: B Chemical.
Maolin Yu (male) is currently a junior in the School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Hunan University of Science and Technology, researching chemosensors and biosensors.

Yunfei Long (male) obtained his Ph.D. degree in Chemistry from Southwest University in 2007. He is currently a Professor at Hunan University of Science and Technology, China. His research interests include synthesis of functional nanomaterials, disease biomarker detection and heavy metal ion determination.

Pinggui Yi (male) is currently a professor in the School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Hunan University of Science and Technology, Xiangtan, China. His primary research interests include functional molecules and materials, proton transfer, chemosensors and biosensors. he has published more than 60 papers in Journal of Physical Chemistry B, Chemical Physics Letters, The Journal of Chemical Physics, and so on.
